content.text: Verifying that individually correct components work correctly when wired together is the core concern of integration testing. In agentic AI systems, where an orchestrator calls an LLM, that LLM emits tool-use requests, and those tools interact with databases, file systems, and web APIs, the wiring between components is often where subtle failures emerge. Integration tests exercise multiple real components end-to-end, catching the class of bugs that unit tests — which substitute every dependency with a mock — cannot reach.

Integration testing for agentic AI systems is the practice of running a real agent — with its full orchestration logic intact — against a set of dependencies that are either real or controlled near-real substitutes. Unlike unit tests, which isolate a single function and mock every external call, integration tests allow two or more components to interact through their actual interfaces. The goal is to confirm that the seams between components — the API contracts, the message formats, the tool-call schemas, the state transitions — all work as designed when real data flows through them.

In practical terms, an integration test for an agentic AI might start a real LLM API call (or a controlled sandbox that mimics it), invoke real database queries against a test-scoped schema, and let the agent's orchestration loop run unmodified. The test observes the final output, the sequence of tool calls made, and the resulting state, then asserts that all three match expectations. The key distinction from end-to-end testing is scope: an integration test exercises a defined boundary — for example, agent plus tool layer, or agent plus memory store — rather than the entire production deployment.

Integration testing sits in the middle of the test pyramid, between fast isolated unit tests at the base and slow full-stack end-to-end tests at the apex. Most mature agentic systems maintain all three layers but invest the most engineering effort in integration tests, because that is where most subtle failures occur in practice.
Agentic AI systems are composed of many independently correct pieces that can still fail at their interfaces. A unit test may verify that the orchestrator correctly parses a
block in isolation — but only an integration test will reveal that the tool function the block names expects a field called
. That mismatch is invisible to unit tests and invisible to the LLM — it only appears when both sides run together.

A second category of integration-only failure is state pollution. When an agent accumulates message history across tool turns, a subtle bug in how tool results are appended to the context may produce correct outputs for the first two turns but cause the third tool call to receive a malformed context. Unit tests, by design, test one function call in isolation; they cannot reproduce a sequence of state mutations that only emerges across multiple turns of a real conversation loop.
Integration tests are also the primary defence against SDK and API schema drift. When the Anthropic Python SDK releases a new minor version that changes the structure of the

type, mocked unit tests — which return pre-built mock objects — continue to pass. An integration test that passes real tool results through the real SDK type system will fail immediately, surfacing the breakage before it reaches production.
Tests confirm that two components agree on field names, data types, and schema structure — the class of bug that unit tests cannot reach because both sides are mocked.
Multi-turn conversation loops accumulate state across calls; integration tests run the full loop and can observe how state evolves from turn to turn, catching mutations that only emerge in sequence.
When a dependency changes its API surface, integration tests fail immediately at the actual call site, pointing to the exact breakage.
Integration tests exercise code paths that actually run in production. Green integration tests are a stronger signal of correctness than green unit tests alone.

A database timeout or a search API rate limit affects not just the tool function but also how the orchestrator handles the error response and whether it retries correctly. Integration tests cover this cross-component behaviour.
When internal modules are reorganised, integration tests verify that the observable behaviour of the assembled system has not changed, even if the internal structure has.

Integration tests are slower and more expensive than unit tests. A test that makes real LLM API calls takes 1–5 seconds per call, and an agent that executes three tool turns will make four or more API calls per test. Running 50 integration tests of this kind can easily consume several minutes and meaningful API budget. This cost is manageable but must be planned for: integration tests typically run on a slower schedule (once per PR merge, or nightly) rather than on every commit.

Non-determinism is the other central challenge. LLM responses vary across calls even at temperature zero, because the model provider may update the underlying model or sampling implementation without a version bump. An integration test that asserts the agent's exact output text will be brittle. Well-designed integration tests instead assert on structural properties: did the agent call the search tool? Did the final response include the required fields? Did the state store contain exactly two tool results? These structural assertions are stable across minor variations in LLM phrasing.
LLM non-determinism, network timeouts, and rate limits all introduce intermittent failures. A flaky integration test suite erodes developer trust and causes real failures to be dismissed as noise.
Integration tests often require a running database, a search service, and API keys. Setting up and tearing down this environment in CI adds infrastructure overhead.
Developers who must wait minutes for integration test results tend to skip running them locally, which delays discovery of integration failures.
Real LLM API calls consume tokens. A large integration suite run many times per day can generate non-trivial monthly API charges.

An integration test for an agentic system follows the standard arrange-act-assert pattern, but the scope of what is "real" is wider than in a unit test. In the arrange phase, the test initialises real components — a real database connection pointed at a test-scoped schema, a real Anthropic client (or a sandbox proxy), and the agent's own orchestration object. In the act phase, the test invokes the agent with a task prompt and waits for it to run to completion, including any LLM calls and tool executions. In the assert phase, the test checks the agent's output and optionally inspects side effects — records written to the database, events emitted to a queue, entries in a log.

The key design choice is which components to keep real and which to replace with controlled substitutes. A common graduation is: (1) keep the LLM real but run against a cheap, fast model such as Haiku; (2) keep tool functions real but pointed at a test database seeded with known data; (3) use a real in-memory state store rather than a production Redis cluster. This combination gives high confidence in interface correctness while keeping costs and latency manageable.

Because integration tests may write to databases or external systems, teardown is as important as setup. After each test, the database must be rolled back or the test schema dropped. Without reliable teardown, tests bleed state into each other, causing ordering-dependent failures that are notoriously difficult to diagnose. pytest fixtures with
are the idiomatic mechanism for this: setup runs before the
, teardown runs after, and pytest guarantees the teardown executes even if the test raises an exception.
Measuring the breadth of integration tests is often expressed in terms of component pair coverage. If the system has components
, integration tests should cover every critical adjacent pair
where data flows across the boundary. The total number of distinct boundaries to cover is at most
, but in practice the relevant boundaries are a small subset of that because most component pairs do not interact directly.

attrs.alt: Integration Testing architecture: Test Harness invokes Agent Under Test, which calls LLM API/Sandbox and External Tools, reads/writes Shared State, and finally returns output to Assertions and Cleanup.

content.text: The diagram shows the five layers of a typical agentic integration test. The
(blue, top) drives the scenario via a standard arrange-act-assert-teardown cycle. It invokes the
(centre), which runs real production code. The agent makes real calls to the
(green, left) — either the real Anthropic endpoint or a deterministic fixture-replay proxy — and dispatches
calls (amber, right) to real or near-real services. Both LLM responses and tool results flow back to the agent. The agent reads and writes
(purple, bottom-centre). When the agent finishes, the harness collects the final output and passes it to
(green, bottom), which verifies correctness and rolls back any state mutations.
Integration tests are the right tool when the failure risk lies at a component boundary rather than inside a single function. Use them in the following scenarios.
The first time a new external API or database is wired into the agent, write an integration test that exercises the full call chain before writing unit tests that mock it.
After bumping the Anthropic SDK version or changing the model identifier, run integration tests to confirm that the message format and tool-call schema are still compatible.
When verifying that state accumulates correctly across a three- or four-turn conversation, integration tests are the only viable approach — unit tests cannot reproduce the sequential state mutations.

Testing that the agent retries a failed database call and eventually succeeds requires the database to actually fail — something a controlled integration test can trigger using a test-scoped fault-injection layer.

High-value business flows — a billing calculation, a compliance check, a document generation pipeline — should have at least one integration test that exercises the real path from user input to final output.

use integration tests as a substitute for unit tests on internal logic. A function that parses a tool-call block should be unit tested with many edge cases; an integration test would only cover the one or two scenarios its harness happens to exercise. The cost of each integration test is high enough that they must be reserved for boundary verification.

Identify the integration boundary to test. List every external system the agent talks to: LLM API, databases, search APIs, file storage. For each, decide whether the integration test will use the real system (pointed at a test environment), a local Docker container, or a deterministic fixture-replay proxy.
Create an isolated test environment. For databases, use a separate test schema or database. For the LLM API, consider using a cheaper model (
) or a fixture-replay proxy that returns recorded real responses. Never run integration tests against the production database.
Write conftest.py fixtures for environment setup and teardown. Use
for expensive operations (starting a Docker container, running database migrations) and
for per-test data setup and rollback.

Seed test data deterministically. Write a seed function or use a factory library that inserts known records before each test and removes them after. The test's assertions depend on this data, so it must be reproducible across environments and CI runs.

Write structural assertions, not text assertions. Assert that the agent called the expected tools in the expected order, that the database contains the expected records, and that the output contains required fields — not that the output is an exact string. Exact-string assertions break on every LLM update.
Separate integration tests from unit tests. Use a dedicated directory (
) so that the fast unit suite and the slower integration suite can be run independently.
Run integration tests in CI on a separate job triggered by PR merge or on a nightly schedule, with appropriate API key secrets and environment variables. Do not run them on every commit push.
Basic: pytest conftest.py for Integration Tests

# tests/integration/conftest.py
# Run with: pytest tests/integration/ -m integration --api-key $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY
#
# Requirements:
#   pip install anthropic pytest pytest-asyncio sqlalchemy

import os
import pytest
import sqlite3
from pathlib import Path
import anthropic


# ── Database fixture ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def test_db(tmp_path_factory):
    """Create a temporary SQLite database with the agent's schema."""
    db_path = tmp_path_factory.mktemp("db") / "test_agent.db"
    conn = sqlite3.connect(str(db_path))
    conn.executescript("""
        CREATE TABLE documents (
            id   INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
            title TEXT NOT NULL,
            body  TEXT NOT NULL,
            tags  TEXT DEFAULT ''
        );
        INSERT INTO documents (title, body, tags)
            VALUES ('Python 3.13 Release Notes',
                    'Python 3.13 introduces a free-threaded mode and JIT preview.',
                    'python,release');
        INSERT INTO documents (title, body, tags)
            VALUES ('Integration Testing Guide',
                    'Integration tests verify component boundaries in real systems.',
                    'testing,quality');
    """)
    conn.commit()
    yield db_path, conn
    conn.close()


# ── LLM client fixture ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def llm_client():
    """Real Anthropic client; skips test suite if no API key is available."""
    api_key = os.environ.get("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY")
    if not api_key:
        pytest.skip("ANTHROPIC_API_KEY not set — skipping integration tests")
    return anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=api_key)


# ── Marker registration ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────

def pytest_configure(config):
    config.addinivalue_line(
        "markers",
        "integration: marks tests as integration tests (real API calls)"
    )
Intermediate: Agent Integration Test with Real LLM Call

Consider a production customer-support agent that accepts user questions, queries a PostgreSQL knowledge base, optionally calls a ticketing API to look up account history, and returns a structured JSON response that the frontend renders. The agent handles roughly 50,000 conversations per month. The engineering team runs three test layers.

The unit layer (300 tests, run in 8 seconds on every commit) mocks both the Anthropic SDK and all external tools. These tests verify parser logic, retry behaviour, prompt assembly, and error-handling code paths in isolation. They have no API cost and produce deterministic results.

The integration layer (25 tests, run on PR merge in approximately 3 minutes) uses a real Haiku model call, a test PostgreSQL schema seeded with 50 representative knowledge-base articles, and a stub ticketing API served by a

instance that returns pre-built JSON. Each test verifies one user journey: account status inquiry, product return request, billing question. Assertions are structural — the response JSON must have the required fields, the knowledge base must have been queried, and the ticket stub must have received the correct account ID.

# Minimal structural assertion pattern used in the production integration suite
import json
import pytest


@pytest.mark.integration
def test_billing_query_returns_structured_response(support_agent, seeded_db, ticket_stub):
    """
    Scenario: user asks about their last invoice.
    Expected flow: agent queries KB, queries ticket system, returns JSON.
    """
    response_json = support_agent.handle(
        user_id="usr_test_001",
        message="What was my last invoice amount?",
    )

    # Parse the structured output the agent is contractually required to produce
    try:
        data = json.loads(response_json)
    except json.JSONDecodeError:
        pytest.fail(f"Agent did not return valid JSON: {response_json!r}")

    # Required field assertions (structural, not text-based)
    assert "answer" in data, "Response must include 'answer' field"
    assert "sources" in data and isinstance(data["sources"], list), (
        "Response must include a 'sources' list"
    )
    assert "confidence" in data and 0.0 <= data["confidence"] <= 1.0, (
        "Confidence must be a float in [0, 1]"
    )

    # Cross-boundary verification: ticket system was queried
    assert ticket_stub.call_count >= 1, (
        "Agent must query the ticket system for billing questions"
    )
    # Verify correct account ID was sent to the ticket system
    last_request_body = json.loads(ticket_stub.last_request.get_data(as_text=True))
    assert last_request_body.get("user_id") == "usr_test_001"

The end-to-end layer (5 tests, run nightly against the staging environment) deploys the actual containerised service and exercises it through the HTTP API, using a dedicated staging Anthropic API key. These tests catch deployment-configuration errors that the integration layer cannot reach.
Asserting on Exact LLM Output Text
break whenever the LLM rephrases its output, which can happen after a model update or even across identical runs at non-zero temperature. Replace exact-string assertions with structural checks:

Using a single database connection across all tests without rolling back writes causes tests to bleed state into each other. A record inserted by test A may appear in test B's results, causing a spurious pass (or fail) that depends entirely on test order. Use database transactions that are rolled back in teardown, or drop and recreate the test schema before each test function.
Testing Too Much in One Test

An integration test that exercises three different user journeys in a single test function is hard to debug when it fails. Each test should exercise one scenario. When the test fails, the failure message should immediately identify which user journey and which boundary misbehaved.
Running Integration Tests on Every Commit

Placing the integration test suite in the same CI job as unit tests causes every commit to wait 3–5 minutes for results and accumulates API costs proportional to commit frequency. Separate the two suites: unit tests run on every push; integration tests run on PR merge or on a schedule. Use a pytest mark (

Integration tests that write to an external service — a staging search index, an object store, a notification queue — and do not clean up after themselves leave the test environment in an unknown state. The next test run starts from a different baseline, making failures non-reproducible. Always pair each write operation in a test with a corresponding delete or rollback in the fixture's teardown path.
Use the cheapest model that exercises the tool-call loop.
For integration tests that are validating interface contracts rather than answer quality,
is an order of magnitude cheaper than Sonnet and fast enough to keep test suites manageable. Reserve Sonnet or Opus for tests that genuinely require more reasoning capability.
Make tests skip gracefully when infrastructure is unavailable.
A developer running tests locally may not have a PostgreSQL instance. Use
in fixtures when required environment variables or service endpoints are missing, rather than failing with a cryptic connection error.
Each test should depend on the smallest dataset needed to exercise its scenario. Large, complex seeds make tests fragile — a change to the seed script can break unrelated tests.
Log the full conversation for debugging.

When an integration test fails, the error message alone rarely explains what happened. Capture the full message history and tool-call sequence and emit them as part of the failure output (using pytest's
An agent stuck in an infinite tool-call loop will hang indefinitely. Use
or a manual timeout wrapper to kill tests that exceed a reasonable maximum (e.g., 60 seconds per test).
Use a fixture-replay proxy for cost-sensitive scenarios.

For tests that must run frequently, record real LLM responses once and replay them on subsequent runs. This eliminates per-run API cost while still exercising the real SDK code path on the initial recording pass.
Assert tool call sequence, not just final output.

A test that only checks the final answer may pass even if the agent took the wrong path to get there. Verify that the agent called the expected tools in the expected order using a call log, to confirm the integration boundary was actually exercised.
The wall-clock time of an integration test is dominated by LLM API latency. A single

call takes 0.5–2 seconds; a three-turn agent loop takes 2–6 seconds per test. A suite of 25 integration tests therefore takes approximately 50–150 seconds — manageable for a PR-merge gate but too slow for per-commit CI.
The expected total duration of an integration suite is approximately:
is the average number of LLM turns per test, and
is the average latency per LLM call. Reducing any factor cuts total suite time proportionally.
Integration tests that use isolated database schemas and stateless LLM calls can be run in parallel with
. On a 4-core CI runner, this can cut wall-clock time by 60–75%.
Tools such as VCR.py or a custom HTTP replay server can eliminate LLM latency for tests whose scenarios have been recorded previously, reducing per-test time from seconds to milliseconds.
Scope expensive fixtures to module or session.
Database migrations and Docker container startup should run once per test session, not once per test function. The
setting amortises setup cost across all tests in the suite.
Set a hard limit on the number of tool turns the agent may execute during a test (e.g.,
). This prevents runaway loops from inflating test duration and API costs when the agent encounters an unexpected scenario.
The standard Python test runner. Its fixture system,
auto-discovery, and mark-based filtering are the foundation of most agentic AI integration suites.
Required when the agent uses the async Anthropic SDK (
). Provides async fixture support and the
decorator to kill runaway tests after N seconds — essential for agentic tests that could loop indefinitely.

Starts a real HTTP server on a local port during a test, allowing the agent to make real HTTP requests to it. Ideal for stubbing external REST APIs (ticket systems, search APIs, webhooks) without writing full mock objects.
Records real HTTP interactions (including LLM API calls) on first run and replays them on subsequent runs. Combines the interface-realism of real HTTP with the speed and cost savings of playback.

Spins up Docker containers (PostgreSQL, Redis, Elasticsearch) from within a pytest fixture, giving integration tests a real service without a pre-existing environment. Tears down the container automatically after the session.
Distributes test execution across multiple CPU cores or remote workers. When integration tests use isolated schemas,
can parallelise them for a significant wall-clock speedup.
Python package includes type definitions for all response shapes. Using
on test code that constructs response objects catches schema mismatches at type-check time, before the test even runs.

Integration tests occupy the critical middle ground. Unit tests run fast and cheaply but cannot catch interface bugs. End-to-end tests catch deployment issues but are expensive and slow. Integration tests strike the balance: real component interactions, controlled environment, tractable cost, and feedback available within a CI pipeline.

Integration tests that use real LLM API keys are a significant secret-management concern. A key committed to source control or leaked through CI logs gives an attacker unrestricted API access. Best practices are: store keys as encrypted CI secrets, never echo them in test output, and use a separate API key scoped to a test project with spending limits set on the Anthropic console.
Never write real customer data to the test database.

Integration tests should use synthetic, generated data only. Real personal information in test fixtures violates data protection regulations and creates a breach vector if the test environment is less hardened than production.

Run the test suite twice in the same environment without a full reset and confirm that the second run produces identical results. If it does not, teardown is incomplete and state is leaking between runs.

Set monthly spending limits on the test API key at the provider level. An infinite-loop bug in an integration test that calls the LLM repeatedly can burn significant budget before CI times out the job.
Isolate the test network from production.

Integration tests that use a test database pointed at the production host or a shared staging environment risk corrupting real data. Use separate hostnames, connection strings, and credentials for test environments.
Monitor for prompt injection in test inputs.

If integration tests construct agent prompts from database rows or external fixtures, a crafted record containing a prompt injection payload could cause the agent to behave unexpectedly. Treat test input data with the same sanitisation vigilance as production input.

Think of an agentic AI system as a chain of plumbing. Each pipe — the LLM client, the tool router, the database adapter, the output formatter — is tested individually with unit tests. Unit tests confirm that each pipe segment is not cracked. But cracks are not the main failure mode in real plumbing: joints are. A joint between two pipes may be misaligned, under-tightened, or made of incompatible materials, and it will leak under real pressure even when each pipe is perfect in isolation.

Integration tests are the pressure tests that run real water through two connected pipe segments to confirm that the joint holds. They do not replace individual pipe inspections (unit tests), and they do not simulate the entire building's water system under full load (end-to-end tests). They target exactly the right thing: the joint — the seam between two components where data crosses a boundary, where a schema must match, where a type must be compatible, where a contract must be honoured.

This framing also explains why integration tests should be few and targeted, not comprehensive. The goal is not to test every path through the assembled system — that is what end-to-end tests are for. The goal is to confirm that each important joint in the plumbing holds under representative conditions. A dozen well-chosen integration tests covering the most critical joints gives far more confidence than a hundred integration tests that overlap with existing unit tests.
Integration tests verify component boundaries — the field-name mismatches, schema drifts, and state-sequence bugs that unit tests with mocks cannot reach.

Keep integration tests structurally assertive, not text-assertive: check that the agent called the right tool, that required fields exist in the output, and that the database contains expected records — not that the LLM phrased its answer identically to last week.
Reliable teardown is mandatory: every write to a database or external service must be reversed after each test, or state bleeds between tests and produces ordering-dependent failures.
Use the cheapest capable model (e.g., claude-3-haiku) for integration tests that are validating interface contracts rather than answer quality, to keep costs and latency manageable.
Separate integration tests from unit tests using pytest marks and run them on PR merge or on a schedule, not on every commit, to avoid inflating CI times and API costs.
Capture the full conversation and tool-call sequence as part of test failure output — without this context, debugging a failing integration test requires reproducing the scenario manually.
Protect test API keys with CI secret management, spending limits, and a dedicated test key separate from production credentials — a leaked key provides unrestricted LLM API access.

The test pyramid principle applies: keep integration tests fewer and more targeted than unit tests; reserve full end-to-end tests for the smallest possible set of critical user journeys verified against a staging deployment.
